title: Grease / Oil on Leather
stainChemistry: Classification: Oil/Grease (non-polar lipid). Grease and cooking oils are non-polar hydrocarbons that penetrate leather's porous structure through capillary action. On pigmented/finished leather, the topcoat slows absorption but grease still migrates through micro-scratches and wear points. On aniline leather, grease absorbs immediately into the unprotected hide, permanently darkening the area. Treatment: absorb excess with talcum powder or cornstarch, then lift residual oil with POG (Paint/Oil/Grease remover) followed by NSD flush and leather conditioning.
whyThisWorks: Grease is a non-polar molecule that won't dissolve in water alone. Absorbent powder draws oil out of the leather by capillary action before it sets. POG emulsifies remaining oil so it can be flushed away with NSD. Conditioning after treatment replaces the natural oils stripped during cleaning, preventing cracking and stiffness.
defaultAssumption: Treat as unknown age / possibly dried.

steps: 1. Apply generous layer of talcum powder or cornstarch over the grease stain. Press gently into the surface without rubbing. Let sit 15 minutes to absorb excess oil from the leather. Brush off with soft brush. Repeat if powder darkens significantly.

2. Apply POG (Paint/Oil/Grease remover) to remaining stain. Blot gently from outside edge toward center — never rub. POG emulsifies the non-polar oil molecules so they release from the leather grain. Replace cloth frequently as it picks up oil.
3. Flush with NSD (Neutral Synthetic Detergent) to remove all POG residue and loosened oil. Use minimal moisture — leather should not be saturated. Blot with clean damp cloth.
4. Rinse with cool water applied via damp cloth — wring cloth nearly dry before use. Blot away all soap residue. Never flood leather with water.
5. Air dry away from direct heat and sunlight. Once fully dry, apply leather conditioner to restore suppleness and replace natural oils lost during treatment. Buff gently with soft cloth.
professionalProtocol.temperature: cool — no heat; heat drives grease deeper into leather and causes drying/cracking
warnings: Using hot water or heat — drives oil deeper into leather grain and causes cracking
Rubbing aggressively — spreads grease and damages leather finish
Using acetone, bleach, or H₂O₂ — strips finish and dye permanently
Skipping conditioning after cleaning — leads to drying and cracking
Using abrasive cloths or scrub pads — scratches leather surface

steps: 1. Blot any excess grease immediately with a clean paper towel. Do not rub — press gently.
2. Cover the stain generously with cornstarch or baking soda. Let it sit for at least 15 minutes (overnight for set stains). The powder absorbs oil from the leather.
3. Brush off the powder with a soft brush. Repeat if the powder absorbed significant oil.

4. Apply a small amount of leather-safe saddle soap or pH-neutral leather cleaner to a soft, damp cloth. Gently work in small circles over the stain — do not scrub. Never use dish soap on aniline or unfinished leather.
5. Wipe clean with a cloth dampened with cool water. Blot dry with a clean towel.
6. Let air dry completely away from heat and sunlight. Apply leather conditioner once dry.
products: Leather-safe saddle soap or pH-neutral leather cleaner
diyProtocol.whenToStop: If the stain persists after two treatment attempts, or if the leather has darkened permanently, take it to a professional leather care specialist.
warnings: Always condition leather after any cleaning — cleaning strips natural oils.
Use a clean, soft cloth — rough materials scratch the leather finish.
Never use acetone, bleach, H₂O₂, or undiluted alcohol on leather.
Never use dish soap on aniline or unfinished leather — it strips the dye.
Never use heat (hair dryer, radiator) to speed drying — causes cracking.

neverDo: Use hot water or heat — drives grease deeper and dries out leather
Use acetone, bleach, H₂O₂, or abrasives — strips finish and dye permanently
Use excess water — causes warping, water spots, and stiffening
Skip conditioning after cleaning — leads to cracking and brittleness
Rub or scrub aggressively — spreads oil and damages finish
fiberSensitivities: Leather: NEVER use acetone, bleach, H₂O₂, alcohol (undiluted), or abrasives. Safe agents: talcum/cornstarch, POG, saddle soap, mild dish soap, leather cleaner. Always condition after cleaning.

escalation.when: Grease has penetrated deeply into aniline/unfinished leather, stain remains after absorbent + POG treatment, or leather has permanently darkened
escalation.whatToTell: Grease/oil stain on leather, treated at home with absorbent powder and mild soap. Oil may have penetrated past the finish. May require professional degreasing or re-dyeing.

contextQuestions: Is the leather finished/pigmented or aniline/unfinished?
How old is the grease stain — fresh or set?
Has heat been applied to the leather (hair dryer, radiator, sunlight)?
What type of grease — cooking oil, motor oil, body oil, butter?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Aniline leather is far more porous than pigmented leather — grease penetrates deeper and may permanently darken the hide. Motor oil is heavier than cooking oil and requires stronger degreasing. Heat exposure after grease contact accelerates permanent bonding. Fresh grease responds well to absorbent treatment; set grease requires professional intervention.
